Client Information

Business Being Sold
Details about the business sale driving the planning strategy.

Deal Architecture — shared by every eligible-gain strategy (QOZ · QSBS · §1042 · §199A · CRT · §1031)

These describe the deal itself. Strategy blocks below read these once — answer them here and every strategy updates. Buyer relationship triggers §1400Z-2(e)(2) related-party + §1202(c)(3) QSBS related-party rules. Industry triggers §1202(e)(3) SSTB exclusion + §199A SSTB phaseout. Competing election overrides QOZ.

Ordinary components — §1245/§1250 recapture, §751 hot assets, customer lists / §197 intangibles, workforce-in-place, accounts receivable, non-compete payments — are not deferrable/excludable. Stock sale = ~100% capital · Asset sale partnership/LLC = 40-80% · Asset sale service business = 30-60% · §1031 RE = 100%.

Sale Proceeds Structure — installment payments, earnouts, clawbacks, escrow holdbacks

The sale-price total above is the gross headline figure. Most HNW deals split it into a cash at close tranche plus deferred / contingent / at-risk components. Each tranche has its own tax timing, QOZ 180-day clock, and risk profile. Cash at close = Sale Price − Installment Note − Earnout (max) − Escrow Holdback − Working-Capital Clawback Risk.

Corporate Financials — operating company P&L · balance sheet · workforce · concentration

Drives QSBS §1202(d) gross-asset gate ($50M pre-OBBBA / $75M post-OBBBA), §831(b) captive sizing (typical premium = 1-3% of revenue), cross-tested PS / DB plan nondiscrimination, §199A QBI calculation, reasonable-compensation analysis, and pre-sale valuation uplift opportunity (current EBITDA × industry multiple vs headline price). Skip if data is unavailable; defaults assume mid-market mix.

▸ Detailed Income Statement — line-item P&L for advisor diligence (optional)
▸ Detailed Balance Sheet — assets, liabilities, equity (optional)
▸ Cash Flow Statement — operating, investing, financing (optional)
▸ Corporate Tax Profile — §174 R&D · §163(j) interest · §250 GILTI/FDII · §41 R&D credit · §172 NOL
▸ Personal Balance Sheet — line-item assets & liabilities for full household net-worth detail (auto-syncs to fields above)

Detail every account, property, and debt for a complete household balance sheet. Each category supports multiple rows. Totals auto-sum + populate the Pre-Sale Net Worth, Other Assets, Mortgage, and Other Debt fields above. Use this when you have a Net Worth Statement or want client-level detail; skip if the simple fields above are enough.

Assets

Cash & Cash Equivalents $0
Taxable Investments & Brokerage $0
Retirement Accounts (IRA · Roth · 401k · HSA — pre-tax basis) $0
Real Estate (FMV — mortgage entered separately below) $0
Business Interests (separate from sale-target above) $0
Personal Property & Collectibles (art, jewelry, vehicles, watches, wine, autographs) $0
Other Assets (life insurance cash value, crypto, deferred comp, notes receivable) $0
TOTAL ASSETS $0

Liabilities

Mortgages $0
Margin Loans & Securities-Backed Lines $0
Other Debt (credit cards, personal loans, lines of credit) $0
TOTAL LIABILITIES $0
HOUSEHOLD NET WORTH $0
Total Inheritance Share
Set each heir's % share — they should add to 100%. Leave 0% if not specifying shares.

Strategy Library
Browse by category or filter by goal. Check a strategy to include it in the plan and configure its parameters.
Browse:
Filter by goal:

Dynasty Trust Parameters

Typically funded with discounted non-voting shares (LLC/C-Corp/family entity) so the economic value transferred exceeds the reported gift value. Grantor typically retains no income rights; beneficiaries (children/grandchildren) receive the trust. If structured as a grantor trust, the grantor pays the trust's income tax each year — an additional tax-free wealth transfer to beneficiaries (Rev. Rul. 2004-64).

CRAT Parameters

DAF Parameters

QOZ Parameters

§1400Z-2 lets you defer eligible capital gains by investing the gain amount (up to the full gain) into a Qualified Opportunity Fund within 180 days of recognition. Deferral runs until the earlier of disposition or 12/31/2026. If held 10+ years, all post-investment appreciation is tax-free (basis steps to FMV on sale). Leave the amount at 0 to default to the remaining CG tax.

Source-side eligibility (buyer relationship · competing election · sale structure · cap-gain % · industry) is set in Section 2 → Deal Architecture. Those answers feed every eligible-gain strategy (QOZ · QSBS · §1042 · §199A · CRT · §1031). The fields below are QOZ-specific only: amount, deferral period, expected return, projection period, QOF investment type, and substantial-improvement plan.

ILIT (Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust) — His / Hers / Joint Policies

Configure up to three policies inside the ILIT: separate policy on the husband, separate policy on the wife, and/or a joint second-to-die survivorship policy. Term policies are pure death benefit; permanent policies (Whole Life, Indexed UL, Variable UL) build cash value and can produce projected income via policy loans / withdrawals. Add a loan-based split-dollar arrangement below the policy section if applicable.

▸ Policy on Primary Client (Husband / Insured 1) — individual coverage
▸ Policy on Spouse (Wife / Insured 2) — individual coverage
▸ Joint Survivorship Policy (Second-to-Die) — pays death benefit only after both insureds have died; cheaper premium because mortality is jointly delayed
▸ Loan-Based Split-Dollar Arrangement (employer/grantor lends premiums to ILIT at AFR; recovers from death benefit)

Loan-regime split-dollar (Reg §1.7872-15) — employer or grantor loans premium dollars to the ILIT at the AFR. Each year's loan is treated as a series of demand loans; ILIT pays interest at AFR or accrues. At death, employer recovers loan + accrued interest; remainder passes to ILIT beneficiaries estate-tax-free. Avoids economic-benefit-regime imputation. Requires written loan agreement + AFR documentation.

Roth Conversion Parameters

GRAT (Grantor Retained Annuity Trust) Parameters

QSBS §1202 Exclusion Parameters

Requires C-Corp stock, originally issued to the holder. Pre-OBBBA (acquired before 7/4/2025): 5+ year hold = 100% exclusion, cap = max($10M, 10× basis), $50M gross-asset ceiling at issuance. Post-OBBBA (7/4/2025+): tiered — 3-yr = 50%, 4-yr = 75%, 5+ yr = 100%; cap = max($15M, 10× basis); $75M gross-asset ceiling. State treatment varies — CA, PA, MS, AL, NJ do not conform.

⚠️ §1202 Eligibility Attestations
Every test below must be satisfied. Any "No" disqualifies the entire §1202 exclusion — the tool zeroes the saving until all four are confirmed.

§1031 Like-Kind Exchange Parameters

Investment real estate only (no primary residences). Replacement property must be identified within 45 days and acquired within 180 days of sale. Cash "boot" taken out is taxable. This defers — not eliminates — the gain.

Oil & Gas Working Interest — §263(c) IDC + §469(c)(3) Active-Loss Treatment

Direct working interest in domestic oil/gas drilling generates an immediate §263(c) Intangible Drilling Cost (IDC) deduction of 60-80% of the investment in Year 1, deductible against ordinary income (W-2, business income, capital gain). The §469(c)(3) working-interest exception exempts WI losses from passive-activity limits — but only if held DIRECTLY or through a vehicle that does not limit liability. LLC blockers and limited-partner positions forfeit the active-loss treatment. TDC (~20%) depreciates over 7 years (§168). Production income is sheltered by §613A percentage depletion (15% of gross). Highly speculative — concentration risk.

§199A Qualified Business Income Deduction Parameters

20% deduction on QBI from pass-through entities (S-Corps, partnerships, LLCs, sole prop). Phase-outs apply above the taxable income threshold (>$241,950 single / >$483,900 joint for 2024). Specified Service Trades or Businesses (SSTB) lose the deduction entirely above the upper threshold.

SLAT (Spousal Lifetime Access Trust) Parameters

Grantor funds an irrevocable trust for the benefit of their spouse; transfer uses lifetime gift/estate exemption. Spouse can request distributions, giving the grantor indirect access while the assets and future growth escape the estate. Reciprocal Trust Doctrine: if both spouses create SLATs for each other, terms must differ materially or IRS may collapse both back into the estate.

IDGT (Intentionally Defective Grantor Trust) Parameters

Grantor sells appreciating assets to the IDGT in exchange for an installment note at the AFR. The trust is a grantor trust for income tax (grantor pays tax on trust income, further reducing the estate) but not for estate tax (trust assets outside the estate). Appreciation above the AFR transfers to beneficiaries gift-tax-free.

Must equal or exceed the §1274(d) AFR for the month of sale. For 9-yr term use mid-term AFR. Note rate below AFR triggers §7872 gift-of-interest recharacterization.

QPRT (Qualified Personal Residence Trust) Parameters

Grantor transfers a primary or one additional residence into an irrevocable trust, retaining the right to live there for a fixed term. At the end of the term, the residence passes to beneficiaries (typically children). The taxable gift is discounted because the grantor's retained use has value.

Mortality risk: if the grantor dies during the term, the residence is pulled back into the estate and the strategy fails. Term should be set aggressively (short) for older grantors. After term, grantor must pay fair-market rent to the trust to continue occupancy — that rent further shifts wealth to beneficiaries.

CRUT (Charitable Remainder Unitrust) Parameters

Like a CRAT, but payouts are a fixed percentage of the trust's current value (re-valued annually) instead of a fixed dollar amount. Payouts grow with trust value — built-in inflation hedge. Minimum 5% unitrust rate, 10% minimum present-value deduction required.

CLT (Charitable Lead Trust) Parameters

Inverse of CRT — charity receives income stream for a fixed term, then remainder passes to heirs. Best when expected growth materially exceeds §7520 rate. Zero-out structures (remainder valued near $0 for gift tax, then outperforming assets transfer tax-free).

Defined Benefit / Cash Balance Plan — §401(a) Qualified Retirement Plan

Owner-friendly qualified plan with much higher contribution limits than 401(k). 2026 §415(b) max benefit ≈ $280K/yr at age 62; that translates to roughly $285K-$320K of annual deductible contribution for an owner age 50-60. Contributions are mandatory once the plan is in place (vs discretionary for profit-sharing) — actuary determines the required amount each year. Stacks on top of a separate 401(k) Profit-Sharing plan for combined $400K+/yr deduction. PBGC coverage exemption available for <25 active participants. Plan termination at sale: roll the balance to an IRA tax-free.

Conservation Easement — §170(h) Charitable Deduction

Donating a perpetual easement (development rights restriction) on land to a qualified land trust generates a charitable deduction equal to the FMV reduction from the restriction. Subject to 50% of AGI limit for individuals (per §170(b)(1)(E) for qualified conservation contributions), 15-year carryforward. The land must have genuine conservation value per §170(h)(4). Plus §2031(c) estate-tax discount up to 40% (max $500K per estate) on the post-easement land remaining in the estate. Avoid syndicated structures: §170(h)(7) caps the deduction at 2.5× basis × pro-rata share for partnership-structured easements (Disaster Tax Relief Act of 2024 codified the prior IRS position from Notice 2017-10), and syndicated easements remain listed transactions with disclosure + 200% accuracy-related penalty exposure.

§1042 ESOP Rollover — C-Corp Owner Capital-Gain Deferral

C-Corp owner sells stock to an ESOP and defers 100% of capital gain by reinvesting proceeds into Qualified Replacement Property (QRP) within 12 months (§1042(c)(4)). Requires the ESOP to own ≥30% of the company post-sale (§1042(b)(2)). Carryover basis from sold stock to QRP — gain recognized when QRP sold (or stepped up at death). §409(n) prohibits seller from participating in ESOP allocations for 10 years. Pair with §1042 + IDGT for "double rollover" / permanent deferral.

Monetized Installment Sale — §453 Note Plus Upfront Loan

Sell appreciated property on a 30-year installment note (§453), then borrow against the note for ~93-95% of the proceeds upfront. Capital gain is deferred until the note matures; interest income on note ≈ interest expense on monetization loan, so cash-flow neutral during the term. IRS focus area: Notice 2023-34 + 2023-65 designate certain monetized installment structures as "transactions of interest" — high audit-disclosure burden. Use only with reputable structurers using arms-length terms.

Domestic Captive Insurance — §831(b) Micro-Captive

Owner forms a separate insurance company that insures specific risks of the operating business. Premiums are deductible to the operating co (up to insurable risk). The captive elects §831(b): premiums up to $2.65M (2024 indexed) are tax-free at the captive level. Capital accumulates inside the captive at corporate (or 0%) tax. IRS focus area: Notice 2016-66 + Avrahami v. Comm'r — micro-captives are listed transactions; require real risk transfer, arms-length pricing, diversified risk pool, qualified actuary, and audited financials.

Premium Finance — Bank-Funded Life Insurance

Bank loans the annual premiums on a large permanent life policy, secured by the policy's cash value plus collateral. Death benefit pays off the loan and leaves net to family. Economics work only when policy crediting rate > loan interest rate by enough margin to cover collateral costs. Highly rate-sensitive; 2024-2026 SOFR+ environment squeezes the spread on indexed-UL policies. §264 limits interest deduction; usually held in ILIT for estate-tax exclusion.

NIMCRUT — Net Income with Make-up Charitable Remainder Unitrust §664(d)(3)

Hybrid CRUT: annual distribution = lesser of (a) the unitrust amount or (b) the trust's actual income. Shortfall years carry forward as a "make-up" liability paid in later high-income years. Powerful when funded with non-income-producing assets (closely-held stock, raw land) — pre-sale years distribute little, post-sale year (when stock sells) distributes large catch-up. Used for income-deferral + estate-removal + charitable deduction in a single structure.

Walton (Zeroed-Out) GRAT — Estate Freeze with Near-Zero Gift Tax

Variant of standard GRAT structured so the annuity factor produces a taxable gift of $0 (per Walton v. Comm'r 115 T.C. 589). All asset appreciation above the §7520 hurdle rate passes to heirs gift-tax-free. Short term (2-3 years) for volatile assets; multiple "rolling" Walton GRATs to compound success. Mortality risk: if grantor dies during term, asset returns to estate (loss of opportunity, not loss of capital).

Domestic Asset Protection Trust (DAPT) — Self-Settled Spendthrift

Self-settled irrevocable trust in a DAPT-friendly state (DE, NV, AK, SD, WY, OH, TN, MO, RI, NH, MS, UT, VA, HI, WV, CT, IN, OK, MI). Grantor is also discretionary beneficiary; trust assets protected from grantor's future creditors after the statute-of-limitations period (typically 18-48 months by state). Pre-existing creditors can still reach if transfer was fraudulent under UVTA. Estate-tax neutral by default (grantor trust); can be drafted as non-grantor for income-tax shifting.

§1045 QSBS Rollover — Defer Gain into New QSBS

If client sells QSBS held <5 years (so §1202 100% exclusion not yet available), §1045 lets them roll proceeds into new QSBS within 60 days. Holding period of original stock tacks onto replacement → continuous 5-yr clock toward §1202. If replacement is held to 5 years, eligible for full §1202 exclusion on combined gain. Used when serial-entrepreneur client has multiple successive ventures.

SCIN — Self-Canceling Installment Note (Estate Removal at Death)

Sale of asset to family member or trust on installment note that cancels at grantor's death. Removes asset from estate at sale date; income/gain still recognized during life as payments received. SCIN premium (above AFR) compensates buyer for cancellation feature — typically priced via mortality tables. Best for grantors with shorter-than-actuarial life expectancy.

Private Placement Life Insurance (PPLI) — UHNW Tax-Free Investment Wrapper

Variable life policy with custom hedge-fund and PE investment options. Internal growth tax-free (§7702 if compliant); death benefit excluded under §101. Available only to accredited investors / qualified purchasers. Onshore (Bermuda Class IV) or offshore (Cayman) carriers. Minimum funding typically $5M+. §817(h) diversification rules require 5+ underlying investments, no investor-control issues (Reg §1.817-5).

Cross-Tested (New Comparability) Profit-Sharing Plan

Profit-sharing variant using new comparability allocation that gives owners a much larger share than rank-and-file employees. Passes §401(a)(4) general test by demonstrating equivalent benefits at retirement age. Stack on top of 401(k) + DB plan for combined $100k+ owner allocation in addition to DB contribution. Best with older owner / younger workforce.

Buy-Sell Agreement — Cross-Purchase / Entity Redemption / Wait-and-See

Contractual obligation to buy/sell business interest at death/disability/departure. Three structures: cross-purchase (other owners buy with personally-owned life insurance), entity redemption (company buys with corporate-owned life insurance), or wait-and-see (decide at trigger). Post-Connelly v. U.S. (2024), corporate-owned life insurance proceeds increase company FMV for §2031 valuation — making entity redemption far less attractive than pre-Connelly.

Family LLC — Pass-Through with Valuation Discount + Charging-Order Protection

LLC variant of FLP — pass-through taxation, governance flexibility, valuation discounts on gifted membership interests (lack of marketability + minority discount), and charging-order protection (creditor of an individual member cannot reach LLC assets, only future distributions to that member's interest). Strongest charging-order statutes: DE / NV / WY. §2036 retained-control issues mirror FLP — engage tax counsel + obtain qualified appraisal at funding.

Intra-Family Loan — AFR-Based Wealth Transfer

Loan from grantor to family member (or grantor trust) at Applicable Federal Rate (AFR). All asset appreciation above AFR transfers gift-tax-free to the borrower over the loan term. Best in low-interest-rate environments. Document the note properly (term, rate, security if any) to avoid §7872 below-market-loan re-characterization. Combine with IDGT for installment-sale-to-grantor-trust at AFR.

Private Foundation — Donor-Controlled Charitable Platform

Donor-controlled grantmaking entity. 30% AGI deduction limit (vs 60% public charity / DAF). 5% annual distribution requirement (§4942). 1.39% net-investment excise tax (§4940 — reduced from 2% in 2020). Excludes self-dealing (§4941), excess business holdings (§4943, <20% of any business), jeopardy investments (§4944), taxable expenditures (§4945). Strong governance + multi-generational platform vs DAF; ideal for $10M+ annual giving budgets.

Funded SERP — Supplemental Executive Retirement Plan

Non-qualified deferred compensation for top-tier executives (typically owner). Funded via corporate-owned life insurance (COLI) or annuities. No §415 contribution limits — discriminatory benefit allowed for "Top-Hat" plans (§401(a)(7) exemption). §409A timing rules apply: deferral elections before year, distribution events fixed in writing. Death benefit excluded under §101 if grantor not a 25%+ owner of the COLI sponsor's stock.

ING Trust — Incomplete Non-Grantor Trust (DING / NING)

Self-settled trust in DE (DING) or NV (NING) that is non-grantor for income tax (assets shift income off donor's state return) but incomplete for gift tax (so it stays in donor's estate — no gift tax). Result: investment income shifted to a no-tax jurisdiction (NV / DE / SD) for state-tax purposes. Powerful only for high-state-tax residents (CA / NY / NJ / OR) with significant investment income. CFTBA conformity issues — California aggressively challenges DING/NING for state income tax; recent legislation has narrowed effectiveness in CA.

HSA — Health Savings Account (Triple-Tax-Advantaged)

Triple-tax-advantaged account: contributions deductible (above-the-line), growth tax-free, qualified-medical withdrawals tax-free. 2024 limits: $4,150 single / $8,300 family + $1,000 catchup at 55+. Requires HDHP coverage (≥$1,600 deductible single / $3,200 family). At 65+, non-medical withdrawals taxed at ordinary rates only (no penalty) — effectively a Traditional IRA backstop. Build long-term medical reserve by paying out-of-pocket and investing the HSA.

Annual Budget & Required Distribution
Client's annual spending translated into a required portfolio distribution rate. Compared against the safe-withdrawal target to flag sustainability risk.

Drives RMD projections (starting at age 73 per SECURE 2.0), Social Security claiming analysis, and feeds the Annual Budget sustainability check.

Current and recommended life insurance coverage.
9
Firm Branding
Upload a logo to replace the default on the plan cover. Default: Pyle Financial Services, Inc.

⚡ Solve Toward Zero — Best Legal Tax Stack

Target: $0 across all four tax buckets — federal income, capital gains, state & local, and estate. True zero isn't achievable on a transaction of this size (QSBS caps at $10M, CRAT 10% remainder test, etc.), but the solver finds the legal combination that gets closest.

The search adds one strategy at a time and keeps it only if it reduces total tax. It respects every qualification check already built in: §1202 attestations, CRAT 10% remainder, QOZ hold period, AGI caps on charitable deductions, recapture treatment.

Realistic outcome: for a $42M sale in Florida, expect a 5-8% effective federal rate (vs. the 23.8% baseline), $0 state, and estate tax driven close to $0 via trust stacking.

Welcome back, —

This is your comprehensive financial plan prepared by your advisor. Browse the tabs to see your strategy, post-sale financials, retirement plan, and estate roadmap. Use Download PDF or Email Plan above to share a copy. Questions? Contact your advisor directly — their details appear in the plan footer.

Comprehensive Financial Plan

—

📋 Meeting Prep Briefing

Pick a meeting type and click Generate. Claude reads the plan + recent reviews and produces a 1-page briefing with snapshot, talking points, red flags, action items, and suggested scripts for hard conversations.
Welcome to Your Plan

A 30-second tour

Your advisor has built a comprehensive financial plan just for you. This quick tour shows you how to get the most from it — or skip if you'd rather explore on your own.

Step 1

Navigate with the tabs

The Client Portrait tab shows your headline numbers. Other tabs dive into retirement, tax strategy, estate planning, and more. Click any tab to explore — nothing breaks if you wander.

Step 2

Ask the AI assistant

See the "Ask Your Plan" button in the bottom-right? Ask it anything — "When can I retire?" "How much will go to my kids?" "Is the Dynasty Trust worth the cost?" It answers in plain English, grounded in your actual numbers.

Step 3

Upload documents & leave notes

Use the Plan Documents panel to send your advisor tax returns or insurance policies securely. Leave a note in the Reviews panel if you have a question between meetings. Your advisor is notified automatically.

You're Set

Any questions, just ask.

This plan is yours — explore it, share it with your CPA or attorney, and come back whenever anything changes in your life. Your advisor will update it for you and send an email when they do.

📄 Tax Document Extraction

Upload a Form 1040 or Schedule K-1 (PDF or image). Claude reads the document and extracts every numeric field. You then review what was extracted and pick which fields to write into the plan. The original file is never stored by us; only the extracted JSON is kept in memory until you apply or close.

Privacy: the extraction prompt explicitly tells Claude not to return SSN, full address, or full EIN. Only last-4 of EIN is retained.

Compare Plan Versions

Pick two saved plans above and click Compare.

📁 Plan Documents

Upload trust instruments, tax returns, insurance policies, engagement letters, and other plan-supporting documents. Files stored securely in Supabase with RLS — team members see all; clients see only their own plan's documents that are marked client-visible.
Every plan create/update/delete, document upload/delete, sign-in, AI query, and PDF export is recorded here. The log is append-only at the database level — even team members cannot edit or delete entries. Use this to defend fiduciary decisions ("when did we ask the client about the SLAT?") and provide SOC 2 Type II evidence of access control.
